name: Application Deployment and Security
overview: This domain covers the practical skills needed to deploy, containerize, and secure network automation applications. You will learn about cloud deployment models, containerization with Docker, CI/CD pipelines, unit testing, application security fundamentals, and DevOps practices. These skills bridge the gap between writing automation scripts and deploying them reliably in production environments.

keyTopics.title: Describe the benefits of edge computing
keyTopics.summary: Edge computing processes data closer to where it is generated rather than sending everything to centralized cloud data centers. This reduces latency and bandwidth usage for time-sensitive network applications.
keyPoints: Edge computing reduces latency by processing data at or near the source instead of in a distant cloud data center
Reduced bandwidth consumption because only processed results or summaries are sent to the cloud, not raw data
Improved reliability for applications that need to function even when cloud connectivity is disrupted
Edge computing is ideal for IoT devices, real-time analytics, and latency-sensitive applications like video surveillance
Cisco IOx and Cisco Edge Intelligence are platforms for deploying applications at the network edge
examTips: Focus on the three main benefits: reduced latency, bandwidth savings, and improved reliability
Know real-world use cases: IoT data processing, video analytics, local caching, and branch office applications
Understand that edge computing complements cloud computing rather than replacing it

keyTopics.title: Identify attributes of different application deployment models
keyTopics.summary: Applications can be deployed across different cloud models depending on requirements for control, security, scalability, and cost. Understanding the attributes of each model helps you choose the right deployment strategy.
keyPoints: Private cloud: Dedicated infrastructure for a single organization, offering maximum control and security but higher cost
Public cloud: Shared infrastructure managed by providers like AWS, Azure, or GCP with pay-as-you-go pricing and elastic scalability
Hybrid cloud: Combines private and public cloud, allowing workloads to move between environments based on requirements
Edge deployment: Computing resources located at or near the data source for low-latency processing
Multi-cloud uses services from multiple public cloud providers to avoid vendor lock-in and leverage best-of-breed services
examTips: Know the trade-offs: private cloud = more control, public cloud = more scalability, hybrid = flexibility
Regulated industries (healthcare, finance) often require private cloud for sensitive data compliance
Edge computing is used when latency or bandwidth constraints make cloud processing impractical

keyTopics.title: Identify the attributes of application deployment types (VMs, bare metal, containers)
keyTopics.summary: Applications can run on bare metal servers, virtual machines, or containers. Each deployment type offers different trade-offs in terms of isolation, resource efficiency, portability, and startup time.
keyPoints: Bare metal: Application runs directly on physical hardware with maximum performance but no resource sharing or isolation
Virtual machines: Full OS virtualization with a hypervisor, providing strong isolation but heavier resource overhead
Containers: Lightweight, process-level isolation sharing the host OS kernel, with fast startup times and minimal overhead
Containers are more portable than VMs because they package the application and its dependencies into a single image
Docker is the most popular container runtime, while Kubernetes orchestrates containers at scale
examTips: Know the key difference: VMs virtualize hardware (each has its own OS), containers virtualize the OS (share the host kernel)
Containers start in seconds while VMs take minutes to boot
VMs provide stronger isolation (separate kernels) while containers are more resource-efficient

keyTopics.title: Describe components for a CI/CD pipeline in application deployments
keyTopics.summary: CI/CD (Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment) automates the process of building, testing, and deploying applications. A well-designed pipeline ensures code quality and enables rapid, reliable releases.
keyPoints: Continuous Integration (CI): Developers merge code frequently, triggering automated builds and tests to catch issues early
Continuous Delivery (CD): Code is automatically prepared for release to production after passing all tests and quality checks
Continuous Deployment: Every change that passes automated tests is automatically deployed to production without manual intervention
Common CI/CD tools: Jenkins, GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, CircleCI, and Drone
Pipeline stages typically include: source (commit), build, test (unit, integration), deploy (staging, production)
examTips: Know the difference between Continuous Delivery (manual approval to deploy) and Continuous Deployment (fully automated)
Understand that CI/CD requires version control (Git) as the trigger for pipeline execution
Be familiar with common pipeline stages and their order: build, test, deploy

keyTopics.title: Construct a Python unit test
keyTopics.summary: Unit tests verify that individual functions and methods work correctly in isolation. Python provides the built-in unittest module and the popular pytest framework for writing and running tests.
keyPoints: Unit tests verify individual functions or methods in isolation from external dependencies
Python unittest requires test classes that inherit from unittest.TestCase with methods prefixed with test_
Common assertions: assertEqual, assertTrue, assertFalse, assertRaises, assertIn, assertIsNone
pytest allows simpler test functions without classes, using plain assert statements
Mocking (unittest.mock) replaces external dependencies with controlled objects for isolated testing
Test fixtures (setUp/tearDown in unittest, fixtures in pytest) prepare and clean up test environments
examTips: Be able to read and write basic unittest test cases with proper assertions
Know the naming convention: test files start with test_, test methods start with test_
Understand when to use mocking: when testing code that depends on external APIs or databases

keyTopics.title: Interpret contents of a Dockerfile
keyTopics.summary: A Dockerfile contains instructions for building a Docker container image. Each instruction creates a layer in the image, defining the base OS, dependencies, application code, and runtime configuration.
keyPoints: FROM specifies the base image (e.g., FROM python:3.11-slim) and must be the first instruction
WORKDIR sets the working directory inside the container for subsequent instructions
COPY and ADD transfer files from the host to the container image (COPY is preferred for simple file copying)
RUN executes commands during the build process (e.g., RUN pip install -r requirements.txt)
EXPOSE documents which ports the container listens on (does not actually publish ports)
CMD specifies the default command to run when the container starts
ENV sets environment variables, and ARG defines build-time variables
examTips: Know the difference between RUN (build-time) and CMD (runtime) instructions
Remember that EXPOSE is documentation only; use -p flag with docker run to actually publish ports
Understand layer caching: place frequently changing instructions (COPY app code) after stable ones (pip install)

keyTopics.title: Utilize Docker images in local developer environment
keyTopics.summary: Docker enables developers to create reproducible development environments using container images. Understanding essential Docker commands for building, running, and managing containers is a critical DevNet skill.
keyPoints: docker build -t myapp:v1 . builds an image from a Dockerfile in the current directory with a tag
docker run -d -p 8080:80 myapp:v1 runs a container in detached mode, mapping host port 8080 to container port 80
docker ps lists running containers, docker ps -a lists all containers including stopped ones
docker images lists all locally stored images with their tags and sizes
docker-compose up starts multi-container applications defined in a docker-compose.yml file
docker pull downloads images from Docker Hub or other container registries
examTips: Know the -d (detached) and -p (port mapping) flags for docker run
Understand the difference between an image (template) and a container (running instance)
Remember that docker-compose.yml defines multi-container applications with networking and volumes

keyTopics.title: Describe application security issues related to secret protection, encryption, and data handling
keyTopics.summary: Securing applications requires protecting sensitive data at rest and in transit, managing secrets properly, and implementing secure data handling practices throughout the development lifecycle.
keyPoints: Never store secrets (API keys, passwords, tokens) in source code or version control repositories
Use environment variables, vault services (HashiCorp Vault), or secret management tools for storing credentials
Encryption at rest protects stored data using algorithms like AES-256
Encryption in transit uses TLS/SSL (HTTPS) to protect data during network transmission
Input validation prevents injection attacks by sanitizing user-provided data before processing
The principle of least privilege ensures applications only have the minimum permissions necessary
examTips: Know the difference between encryption at rest (stored data) and encryption in transit (network data)
Remember that HTTPS uses TLS to encrypt HTTP traffic between client and server
Understand why .gitignore should include .env files and credential files

keyTopics.title: Explain how firewall, DNS, load balancers, and reverse proxy in application deployment
keyTopics.summary: Production application deployments rely on several infrastructure components to ensure security, availability, and performance. Understanding the role of each component in the deployment architecture is essential.
keyPoints: Firewalls filter network traffic based on rules to protect applications from unauthorized access and attacks
DNS translates domain names to IP addresses, enabling users to reach applications by name rather than IP
Load balancers distribute incoming traffic across multiple application instances for scalability and high availability
Reverse proxies sit in front of application servers, handling SSL termination, caching, and request routing
Common load balancing algorithms include round-robin, least connections, and IP hash
examTips: Know the difference between a forward proxy (client-side) and a reverse proxy (server-side)
Understand that load balancers enable horizontal scaling by distributing traffic across multiple servers
DNS is often the first point of failure in application connectivity issues

keyTopics.title: Describe top OWASP threats (XSS, SQL injections, and CSRF)
keyTopics.summary: The OWASP Top 10 identifies the most critical web application security risks. Understanding these threats is essential for building secure automation tools and web interfaces.
keyPoints: SQL Injection: Attacker inserts malicious SQL code into input fields to manipulate database queries
Cross-Site Scripting (XSS): Attacker injects malicious JavaScript into web pages viewed by other users
Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF): Attacker tricks authenticated users into performing unintended actions
Prevention: Use parameterized queries (SQL injection), input sanitization and output encoding (XSS), and CSRF tokens
The OWASP Top 10 is updated periodically and includes additional threats like broken authentication and security misconfiguration
examTips: Know the attack vector and prevention method for each: SQL injection (parameterized queries), XSS (output encoding), CSRF (anti-CSRF tokens)
Understand that SQL injection targets the database, XSS targets the browser/user, and CSRF targets authenticated sessions
Input validation alone is not sufficient; defense-in-depth with multiple layers of protection is required

keyTopics.title: Utilize Bash commands (file management, directory navigation, and environment variables)
keyTopics.summary: Bash scripting is fundamental for DevOps tasks, automation scripts, and CI/CD pipelines. Mastering essential Bash commands for file operations, navigation, and environment management is a core DevNet skill.
keyPoints: Navigation: cd (change directory), pwd (print working directory), ls (list files), ls -la (list all with details)
File management: cp (copy), mv (move/rename), rm (remove), mkdir (create directory), touch (create empty file)
Environment variables: export VAR=value sets a variable, echo $VAR reads it, env lists all variables
Piping (|) sends output of one command as input to another; redirection (>, >>) writes output to files
chmod changes file permissions, chown changes file ownership, grep searches file contents
examTips: Know the most common commands: cd, ls, cp, mv, rm, mkdir, cat, grep, chmod, export
Understand environment variables and how they are used to pass configuration to applications
Remember that export makes a variable available to child processes

keyTopics.title: Describe the principles of DevOps practices
keyTopics.summary: DevOps is a cultural and technical approach that unifies software development (Dev) and IT operations (Ops) to deliver applications faster, more reliably, and with continuous improvement.
keyPoints: DevOps emphasizes collaboration between development and operations teams to break down silos
Infrastructure as Code (IaC) manages infrastructure through version-controlled configuration files rather than manual processes
Continuous monitoring provides real-time feedback on application performance and infrastructure health
Automation of build, test, and deployment processes reduces manual errors and accelerates delivery
The DevOps lifecycle includes: Plan, Code, Build, Test, Release, Deploy, Operate, and Monitor
examTips: DevOps is a culture and set of practices, not a specific tool or technology
Know the relationship between DevOps, CI/CD, and Infrastructure as Code
Understand that DevOps applies to network automation (NetDevOps) as well as traditional application development

resources.title: Cisco DevNet - DevOps for Network Engineers

practiceScenarios: You are deploying a network monitoring application. Compare deploying it on a VM versus in a Docker container, considering startup time, resource usage, and portability.
Write a Dockerfile for a Python Flask application that serves a network device inventory API. Include installing dependencies from requirements.txt and exposing port 5000.
Design a CI/CD pipeline for a network automation repository that runs unit tests, builds a Docker image, and deploys to a staging environment on every push to the main branch.
Your web-based network dashboard has a form that queries devices by name. Explain how an attacker could exploit SQL injection and how you would prevent it.
commonMistakes: Confusing Docker images (read-only templates) with Docker containers (running instances of images)
Using EXPOSE in a Dockerfile and expecting ports to be accessible without the -p flag in docker run
Storing API keys and passwords directly in Dockerfiles or source code instead of using environment variables or secrets
Confusing Continuous Delivery (requires manual approval to deploy) with Continuous Deployment (fully automated)
Not understanding that containers share the host OS kernel while VMs have their own kernel, affecting isolation level
